data.title: Adamas Trust: Mortgage REIT Preferred Share Floating At 10.8% Yield
data.description:
Introduction

Adamas Trust, Inc. (ADAM), formerly known as New York Mortgage Trust, is am mREIT, or a real estate investment trust that invests primarily in mortgages. Along with the common shares, the company offers four preferred shares and four baby bonds. While all these securities offer attractive yields, one preferred share (ADAMM) is floating above the others and currently offering a 10.8% dividend yield. I last wrote about the company and its baby bonds back in August. Today, I’m not the biggest fan of the preferred shares, but the 10.8% yield offers the best premium to income investors for the risks faced by the company.

Adamas Trust Earnings Results

The pandemic did significant damage to Adamas’ balance sheet. To avoid additional problems, the company sold a significant amount of its mortgages in the first quarter of 2020. After the mortgage market stabilized and interest rates plummeted, the company began steadily re-growing its investment portfolio.

Like many mREITs, Adamas once again found itself in a predicament in 2023. After acquiring several pandemic era mortgages at low fixed interest rates, the company saw its borrowing costs rise sharply as the Federal Reserve moved to increase short-term interest rates to battle rising inflation. Fortunately, unlike many of its peers, borrowing costs never exceeded asset yields. As interest rates fell, net interest spreads (asset yield less financing cost) began to slowly rise and currently stands at 1.50%.

Lower interest rates have not hurt Adamas’ ability to grow income. Interest income has grown steadily from quarter to quarter for more than two consecutive years, surpassing $160 million in the third quarter. Interest expense has also increased despite the drop in interest rates. In fact, in the third quarter alone, interest expense rose by nearly $20 million to $124 million.

The growth in interest income and expense is attributed to the explosive growth in the investment portfolio. Adamas has managed to continue to grow net interest income (interest income less interest expense) as it expands its portfolio in the face of lower interest rates. Net interest income edged higher in the third quarter to $36.5 million, just $1 million shy of where it was in 2021 before interest rates rose and eroded profits.

Risks To Adamas

The company’s growth trajectory lies behind the bet that interest rates will not rise. To highlight how much of a bet Adamas has taken, look at the portfolio recourse leverage, which has grown notably over the last few quarters. Higher leverage can place enormous pressure on the company’s earnings if interest rates revert higher. While Adamas has seen relief from short-term rates, long-term interest rates remain expensive, as the company recently priced a 9.25% five year baby bond.

Added to the complication of leverage is the liquidity situation for Adamas. While liquidity has remained stable in the $400 million to $500 million range, it has stayed flat against the backdrop of a growing balance sheet. Having a $4 billion investment portfolio go into distress in 2021 with $400 million in liquidity is materially different than a $10 billion investment portfolio with the same level of liquidity. The shrinking liquidity relative to the portfolio could become a problem, and management should try to address it by increasing liquidity in future quarters.

Why Not Common Shares?

Interestingly, the recent rally in Adamas’ common shares has created a dividend yield that is lower than ADAMM. But, even if the common shares sell off to a higher dividend yield, I will not advise investors to take a position. Adamas has consistently struggled to deliver shareholder value, with book value per share on a continuous decline over the last six years. It also doesn’t help that there have been three dividend cuts in the last ten years. It’s clear that if the risks mentioned come to fruition, the common shareholders will pay the price.

What About Baby Bonds?

Adamas has four different baby bonds, following the recent issuance of ADAMO. The yields on these bonds range from 8.7% to 9.25%. These are solid yields and for investors who are more conservative or have a lower risk tolerance, but the spread of 120 basis points in income yields between the baby bonds and ADAMM is too great to ignore. While the preferred shares are higher risk, I believe the higher yield is sufficient to compensate investors willing to take that risk. It’s also important to note that the common dividends of Adamas would need to be eliminated before the preferred dividends can be impaired.

Conclusion

While lower interest rates will help Adamas Trust, I’m doubtful it will be sufficient to support an investment in the common shares. One of the preferred shares is offering a 10.8% dividend yield, which is where I think the best income opportunity lies. The preferred shares are callable, and while that may mean redemption sooner rather than later, it also acts as a buffer against any short-term pricing pressure. Between the nine income options offered by Adamas, I believe that ADAMM is the most attractive.

data.title: Penske Automotive Group: A Mixed Bag That I'm Not Ready To Touch
data.description:
Back in late October of last year, I decided to downgrade Penske Automotive Group, Inc. (PAG) from a "Buy" to a "Hold." I did not make this decision lightly. However, I was turned off by mixed financial performance leading up to that point. On top of this, during the time that I had been bullish about the institution, shares had risen drastically. They increased an astounding 88.3% from the time that I originally rated it a "Buy" in October 2021 until I subsequently downgraded it in October of last year. The market, as defined by the S&P 500, meanwhile, appreciated a more modest 53.8%. This move higher, combined with weakening fundamentals, led me to take a more neutral stance. And since then, the stock has fallen by 2.2% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 3.9%.

Looking at the data today, I see a company that is slowly but surely still growing revenue. However, profitability remains a mixed bag, and some of the most important profit metrics have been weakening. Challenges in this sector are proving to be a problem for the company. And in all likelihood, we should expect this trend to continue into 2026. If the stock was cheap enough, I would feel fine taking a more neutral stance on it. But given the overall valuation on an absolute basis and compared to other players in the automotive retail space, I believe that maintaining my "Hold" rating makes the most sense.

Not Buying This One

Only one additional operating quarter has seen data reported since I last wrote about Penske Automotive Group late last year. That data covers the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. According to management, overall revenue for that time was just a hair under $7.70 billion. That compares favorably to the $7.59 billion that the institution reported a year earlier. Any sort of growth might seem positive. However, context is important here.

According to management, when we look at the Retail Automotive Dealership operations of the company, we have seen some weakness. New vehicle unit retail sales have dropped 1.4% from 49,529 to 48,809. It is worth mentioning that new agency unit sales actually increased nicely from 10,290 to 11,730. That's a 14% increase. And that was instrumental in pushing up new sales revenue for the company to $2.96 billion compared to the $2.89 billion reported a year earlier.

I do think that a bit of detail is required here because this is highly nuanced. When it comes to new vehicles, the company has a couple of different ways that it sells them. Under its traditional retail model, it purchases the inventory and then sells that inventory directly to customers. In this case, the company books all the vehicle revenue associated with that sale. But it also has its agency model, which is used by luxury OEMs. In this case, the OEM owns the vehicle while the dealer, in this case Penske Automotive Group, only serves as the sales agent. It appears as though the surge in buying under this agency category can be chalked up to not only stronger demand for luxury brands, which would make sense considering that wealthier individuals would have been more able to buy new vehicles ahead of continued tariff pressure before the year was out, but also because a lot of the electric vehicles that it sells fall under this category also. And at the end of the month of September of last year, the $7,500 electric vehicle tax credit expired, and there was documented demand growth leading up to that point. All of this makes sense conceptually.

When it comes to used vehicles, the picture for the company was interesting to say the least. Actual unit sales dropped 5.2% from 57,738 to 54,708. However, overall revenue for used retail units grew from $2.12 billion to $2.24 billion. This was because of an 11.4% surge in the average retail sales price per used vehicle from $36,785 to $40,995. It does appear as though at least a good part of this improvement came not only from a decline in the supply of used vehicles in the United States that allowed companies such as Penske Automotive Group to push prices higher, but also because of changes made in its operations in the UK that focused on higher-priced and higher-margin inventory.

There are other aspects of the company as well. For instance, the business has its own Retail Automotive Dealership Service and Parts operation, which saw revenue expand from $778 million to $818.3 million year over year. This rise in sales was driven by multiple factors, including net dealership acquisitions. However, the company also benefited from a rise in customer pay revenue, greater warranty revenue, and additional vehicle preparation and body shop revenue. In essence, management is of the opinion that the company has been the beneficiary of an aging fleet. The average age of a vehicle in the United States continues to increase over time, some of which can be attributed to improvements in technology and some of which is almost certainly because of budgetary issues that consumers are having in face of years of rising prices. So this makes sense here.

Unfortunately, the big area of weakness for the company involved its Retail Commercial Truck Dealership operations. New retail sales revenue plummeted 17.8% from $755.3 million down to $620.8 million. This was the result of a 20.5% plunge in the number of units sold from 5,405 vehicles down to 4,298. This one does not shock me. We have been in a prolonged period of weakness in the freight industry. This follows a surge in activity caused by the COVID-19 pandemic. But over investment and the economic slowdown that eventually developed into what we have today naturally caused pain for this space.

With revenue for the company rising, you would expect that profitability would improve as well. But that's not what transpired. In fact, net income actually fell from $226.1 million to $213 million. Certain operational costs, like selling, general, and administrative expenses, increased. Margins have also contracted in key areas because of broader market issues. Management mentioned in their third quarter earnings call that tariffs proved problematic. However, they did not quantify the extent of that pain. Other profitability metrics largely worsened also. Adjusted net profits saw a very similar path as net profits. It is true that operating cash flow for the company jumped from $271 million to $379.5 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $248.1 million to $310.6 million. On the other hand, EBITDA for the company contracted from $367.9 million to $357.1 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Revenue increased. And actually, net profits and adjusted net profits increased as well. But some of the cash flow metrics for the company worsened. What this shows is that while there has been some weakness for a while, the third quarter on its own showed an acceleration of the pain that the company is exhibiting. But that's not stopping management from continuing to focus on the long haul. And management is not afraid to get innovative on that front. For instance, in Australia, the firm continues to focus on energy solutions activities. This particular part of the business involves the provision of power systems for large data centers, mining, and military applications. And even though it might seem odd, it falls specifically under the firm’s Commercial Vehicle Distribution and Other segment. According to management, this piece of the pie alone is expected to grow to $1 billion in revenue by the year 2030. Considering that, in the first nine months of 2025, the segment that this falls under was responsible for $619.3 million, and that it includes other assets in it, I would say that this is a rather significant potential revenue driver for the business.

This certainly helps to inform my overall view of the company. However, unless we actually start to see some big traction on that front, I think it's too early to turn bullish. If we project our results for the company based on what we have seen already for the 2025 fiscal year, we would expect adjusted net profits to come in at $928.1 million. Adjusted operating cash flow would be $987.5 million, while EBITDA would amount to $1.52 billion.

Using these estimates, as well as the results that the company achieved for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced. These multiples aren't bad, but they also don't make the company the most attractive thing out there. If anything, they put it near the low end of the fair value range. And honestly, that chart also confirms this on a relative basis. In it, you can see how the enterprise stacks up against five other similar firms. On a price-to-earnings basis, two of the companies are cheaper. And when it comes to the other two ways of valuing the business, three of them are cheaper.

Takeaway

As much as I would love to turn bullish on Penske Automotive Group, I doubt that makes sense at this time. The company had a miraculous run for a couple of years. Revenue is continuing to grow, but profitability has taken a bit of a hit. If the stock were cheaper, I would feel more optimistic. But as things stand, I believe that maintaining it as a "Hold" candidate is the right move.

data.title: Why I Am Bullish On Gaming, But Bearish On Roblox
data.description:
Introduction

Entertainment is not a new area for me to break down, but gaming is. For example, my latest breakdown was Netflix (NFLX), after reports that the company had explored a potential acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD). I wrote an article on that situation, and I wasn’t happy with how the whole setup unfolded.

But gaming is not like that. This is a huge industry for me, and I am genuinely bullish on it. I also like games myself, so much so that my husband bought me a PlayStation 5 for Christmas, and I am beyond myself, because I had really wanted that console, but what is even better, he promised to play games with me. Now this is something we do together from time to time. If you have never played yourself, I suggest trying. It’s really fun.

Anyway, back to our discussion, I feel I can understand the industry not only from an investor’s perspective, but also from the standpoint of an actual consumer, which would help Roblox Corporation (RBLX) to get a buy rating from me. But, there is another side of me that looks at gaming, and Roblox in particular, through a very different lens - as a parent. I have a stepson, my husband’s son, who is nine years old, so he is Roblox’s main target audience. He and his friends spend a significant amount of time on that platform. We try to limit that exposure, but unfortunately, and to my personal disappointment, it is extremely difficult to do so because all of his friends are there.

My Opinion About Roblox

At some point, my husband and I decided to try Roblox to see what was actually going on inside the platform, so we don’t just blindly agree for the kid to spend the time there. After using it, based on my personal experience, I think this isn’t a healthy environment for children. Even though the company is under pressure and it looks like it is making efforts to improve that, I don’t think that it’s enough.

For instance, look at the new rules for using the microphone. Roblox started requiring photo verification for making sure the age of the user is suitable for chat use, which is an okay attempt, but I see two major flaws here. First, verifications like that rarely stop people with malicious intent. They could find a loophole. Second, and this is the part that upsets parents, we are now expected to upload photos of our kids to the cloud. They say that it goes to Persona, which deletes the data immediately, but hackers get only better and better, and it’s hard to trust those apps with kids' sensitive data. We worry about our own credit cards and identities being stolen constantly. Why would we want to expose our children to that same danger just so they can chat in a game?

Beyond chat functionality, some of the content on Roblox itself raises red flags for me. Certain games are strangely sexualized (I saw it myself), and in my view, this is the most important thing we need to know about Roblox. It definitely looks like a lack of care when it comes to child safety, especially when the sexualized content requires an additional payment to get access. I would be strongly against my child using a platform where this type of content is easily accessible. Roblox is clearly positioned as a children’s platform, yet from a safety standpoint, I do not believe it is suitable for kids at all.

Then, there is an argument that adults could be using it, but I, personally, can’t really imagine myself, or anyone my age, playing Roblox. I see no fun for adults to play Roblox with children, because most likely, you will be the only adult in the game.

You could argue that I am too biased to analyze this company objectively, but I disagree. Investors typically gravitate toward industries or businesses they understand, like, or find useful. They invest in companies where they believe the business model works, or where there is an edge, or where long-term value creation is possible, etc. Roblox’s business model is not bad, but my issue is with the company's policy regarding safety. And this is a major risk, because there are countries that banned Roblox, and I think (or I wish) that can continue happening.

Q3 2025

Looking at results for the previous quarter, Roblox did everything right and managed to surprise the market to the upside. The company is currently experiencing a strong surge in both users and monetization. At the same time, 2026 looks set to be a year of margin pressure. Roblox is spending aggressively on AI infrastructure in an effort to maintain its dominance beyond 2026, and that spending is not cheap. Realistically, this suggests that the stock could remain relatively flat over the coming year.

From a financial standpoint, bookings, or the company’s real revenue, came in at $1.9 billion, up 70% year over year and roughly $220 million above analyst expectations. Even with the growth like this, the company remains unprofitable. It had a loss per share of about $0.37, which was better than expected by $0.14, but it is still a loss, and there is no clear path to near-term profitability.

Management has emphasized that the platform is “aging up,” noting that the 13-plus demographic now represents roughly two-thirds of users (101 million compared to almost 50 million). This is where my skepticism becomes very strong. Based on what I have personally observed, many children (including my husband’s son and his friends) have 13-plus accounts simply because they want to communicate while playing. Roblox restricts chat functionality for younger accounts, so parents often create older accounts for their children using their own email addresses.

Can I prove this or find any statistics on this? Of course not. But at the same time, Roblox also has no way to track it. These kids talk constantly while playing, which means they are using 13-plus accounts, so this does not necessarily mean the platform is organically aging up. And I think parents will continue helping kids, because it’s an impossible decision to make. The child may never encounter anything bad there, but if you cut him off, he will be an outsider in the group, because kids nowadays communicate in games. You always want to do what feels best for your child, even if you are uncomfortable with the platform itself.

Back to the numbers. DAUs, or daily active users, surged more than 70% year over year to 150 million, a record growth as of late. Asia-Pacific growth was very strong at 100%.

On the earnings call, management focused heavily on its technology moat and AI infrastructure. The CEO said Roblox is not just a gaming company but also a data and AI company. To be fair, nearly every company today wants to position itself that way. Management said that Roblox processes about 30,000 years of human interaction data every day, using this information to train internal AI models for safety, movement, and behavior.

The company is also rolling out tools that allow creators to generate 3D objects and interactions in real time using AI, which is an interesting thing. I wonder how well that will be operating. Roblox is also shifting toward a hybrid infrastructure model that combines its own data centers with cloud services. This, by the way, will be the reason for margin compression going forward (high spending on GPUs, infrastructure, etc.), and this is one of the reasons for the stock drop in the last few months.

Valuation

We are now approaching earnings on February 5th, and the stock has dropped well over 7% so far today.

While that is a significant move, the valuation is still very high. At more than 9 times forward sales and over 13 times trailing twelve-month price-to-sales, Roblox is expensive. It is cheaper than it was throughout the good part of 2025, but it is far from cheap.

Can the stock fall further? Absolutely, especially if the performance is weaker than expected or if the market drops on some news that could not even be related to Roblox, like, for example, today. Because of the high valuation and the fact that this company is unprofitable, investors tend to sell stocks like this in uncertain times.

The company has sufficient cash and is not going to shut down or something dramatic like that, but it continues to dilute shareholders. For all the reasons discussed in this article, analysts are not bullish ahead of Q4 earnings that are less than 2 weeks from now. They gave eight negative earnings revisions versus just one positive revision.

Conclusion

More broadly, I do believe AI could be a major catalyst for the gaming industry as a whole. As AI improves, games will become far more interesting and real, and I think that even people who do not consider themselves gamers will want to try out those games.

The company indeed has massive top-line growth, and it is beating expectations by hundreds of millions of dollars. Their user base is expanding globally at an incredible rate. But this is also an unprofitable company that is trading at a very high multiple and facing significant headwinds in terms of safety, regulation, lawsuits, and margin compression.

As a parent, I find it difficult to support the product itself, and as an adult, I don’t find this interesting for myself. I cannot separate the investor from the consumer in this case. Investing is personal, and you have to believe in the story you are buying. With Roblox, the financial story is getting messy with rising costs, and the human story is getting messy with safety. I don’t see how I would be investing in Roblox if the company did not seriously review its approach to content and audience on the platform.

data.title: New Year, Same Story: Geopolitics, Tariffs, And Resilience
data.description:
By Brian Levitt, Chief Global Market Strategist and Head of Strategy & Insights and Benjamin Jones, Global Head of Research, Strategy & Insights

“Nothing changes on New Year’s Day.” This famous line from a 1983 U2 song captures a truth often overlooked in financial markets — while the calendar may flip, the underlying macro and market trends rarely undergo dramatic shifts overnight. 2026 has begun much like 2025 ended: Broadening of markets continued, as global stock markets continued their upward trajectory,1 supported by expected strong earnings growth,2 anchored inflation expectations,3 and optimism around potential central bank policy easing.4

Yet, as was the case last year, investors face a proverbial “wall of worry,” none of which we believe is likely to derail the market advance. These include, but aren’t limited to: Persistent geopolitical tensions and new flashpoints such as the developments in Venezuela, Greenland, and Iran, questions surrounding the independence of the Federal Reserve (Fed), and the legality of the Trump administration’s tariffs.

Meanwhile, the US economy enters the year on a sound footing, resilient, and with important signs that productivity has improved.5

Concerns over Fed independence return

The Trump administration’s Department of Justice is opening an investigation into the US Fed and raised the possibility of a criminal indictment connected to Fed Chair Jerome Powell’s testimony regarding cost overruns associated with the Fed’s building renovation. Powell pushed back publicly and strongly, releasing a video in which he argued that the building costs are merely a pretext.

The independence of the central bank is critical. That should not be a bold or controversial statement. It is a foundational principle of modern macroeconomic management and a cornerstone of financial market confidence. The market reaction to this news has been muted. Stocks were generally stable,6 and the US dollar surprisingly advanced in the week.7 We believe that the muted reaction is justified for now as the market appears to have grown accustomed to the jawboning from the administration. It also raises the probability that Chair Powell will remain on the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) even after his term as Chair ends. He would be the third Chairman to break convention and do that. Ironically, the upshot could be a Fed that is more hawkish than was previously expected. This is an important and changing story. We’ll be watching inflation expectations closely. They remain within the Fed’s perceived “comfort zone,” despite a sizeable increase early in the week.8

Energy markets remain stable despite Venezuela conflict

Recent events in Venezuela and Iran have made big headlines. Questions about potential global impacts are being raised. Typically, geopolitical developments have only disrupted markets when they either damage global economic activity — via a growth or supply shock — or provoke a shift in policy by the world’s major central banks. In Venezuela’s case, neither outcome seems likely. The same is true in the Middle East, provided the Strait of Hormuz remains open.

Venezuela’s diminished oil production capacity9 and the overall robustness of world oil supply10 have kept energy markets stable. While Venezuela reportedly has more oil reserves than Saudi Arabia, there’s little likelihood that Venezuelan production rises meaningfully for years at best. Thus, the muted reaction in oil makes sense to us.

Unsurprisingly, precious metals, including gold and silver, have advanced11 as investors sought potential safe havens. We think both have the potential to move higher if geopolitical concerns, including those in Iran and Greenland, persist.

Supreme Court delays tariff ruling

The US Supreme Court delayed its ruling on the legality of the tariffs that President Trump issued last year under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). A ruling is expected in the coming weeks. Regardless, overall US tariff levels are unlikely to decline substantially even if the IEEPA tariffs are struck down. Other statutes grant the president broad authority to impose tariffs, meaning those previously enacted under the IEEPA could be reimposed under different legal frameworks.

Tariff pressure on Europe over Greenland

Over the weekend, President Trump announced that several European countries would face additional tariffs if they didn’t support the full sale of Greenland to the US. President Trump’s rhetoric around a sale then grew with a letter sent to the Norwegian Prime Minister on Monday. He threatened to apply a 10% tariffs on all goods exported from Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the UK, the Netherlands, and Finland to the US starting February 1 until a deal is reached for the “Complete and Total Purchase of Greenland.” Also, the tariff will be increased to 25% on June 1, 2026, if a deal hasn’t been reached. This is on top of existing tariffs. European Union (EU) leaders are debating their response, but one option could include invoking the Anti-Coercion Instrument (ACI), which would allow the EU to close off US access to the European single market.

Markets have reacted in a relatively sanguine fashion, and for now, we think that’s probably the right and expected reaction. We knew the US administration wanted to acquire Greenland, and President Trump has a clear history of threatening high tariffs and then walking them back. These moves support our core views: A weaker US dollar, higher precious metals prices, and potential outperformance by non-US stocks.

US banks deliver, but fall on credit card risks

US banks were the worst-performing group in the US last week despite delivering strong earnings results that painted a picture of a resilient US consumer and strong trading revenue.12 Downward pressure came as a result of President Trump calling for a 10% cap on credit card rates — average credit card rates have never been at 10%.13 It remains to be seen whether the US administration could deliver this change and how bank credit would respond. But the idea sends a message. The US administration wants a very strong economy this year and will try unconventional measures to get it.

Data illustrate the US economy’s resilience

Recent data releases served as a reminder that the US economy has entered 2026 much as it ended 2025 — on a sound footing with resilient, indeed, improving growth.

  • Manufacturing survey data held steady but remains in contraction territory,14 while services pointed to expansion, albeit at a moderate pace.15
  • Businesses continued to show caution, reluctant to hire aggressively16 yet unwilling to cut staff, as jobless claims stay near historic lows.17
  • Credit card spending data18 underscored that consumers are still spending, supporting overall stability.
  • Inflation is elevated but relatively stable.19
  • Importantly, third-quarter productivity came in at 4.9%,20 perhaps a sign that the benefits of new technologies such as artificial intelligence (AI) are beginning to accrue. We view this as bullish for stocks, as rising productivity is supportive for corporate earnings and allows the central bankers to run the economy hot without inflation.
  • The latest Atlanta Fed GDP Nowcast figure is pointing to real US GDP growth of near 5.5%.21

Our outlook remains the same

The restful holiday period seems a long time ago now, and while it might feel like a lot is changing, we are content that our core views haven’t changed. If anything, they are strengthened. We still believe growth will improve globally and stocks have the potential to rise, led by non-US markets and cyclical sectors.
