data.title: ORIC Pharmaceuticals: Transition To Late-Stage Development Presents Upside
data.description:
While the majority of the biotech industry focuses on developing new modalities, ORIC Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ORIC) has taken a different path; they have focused on developing small molecule inhibitors that represent the best in class within proven, yet challenging to treat, pathways. As we begin 2026, ORIC is poised to cross the line from an early-stage clinical developer to a late-stage clinical developer, with two distinct product candidates (both small molecules), each positioned to enter registrational trials. My thesis regarding ORIC is based on their systematic approach to addressing therapeutic resistance in cancer, a focus area representing one of the biggest reasons why treatments fail in oncology.

Financial And Operational Update

The company's latest update on their balance sheet reported around $413 million in cash, cash equivalents, and investments. This large cash reserve is a direct result of a very successful equity offering of $244 million during 2025, with involvement from top-tier healthcare specialist funds. This cash position should not be understated. ORIC has indicated that this level of cash will allow them to fund their operation into the second half of 2028. As such, it represents a strategic asset to ORIC as a pre-commercial biotech. With this length of time for funding, the company's ability to initiate Phase 3 trials, and achieve the primary endpoint readout of the first Phase 3 trial for rinzimetostat, the company’s pipeline is secure and clear for at least another 2 years.

Therefore, unlike many of their peer companies that must raise capital based upon the completion of Phase 2 trials, regardless of market conditions, ORIC has the luxury of conducting their clinical development program without the need to immediately raise additional capital. This level of financial flexibility will enable the ORIC management team to focus on creating long-term value, rather than being forced to create short-term upside, as evidenced by their deliberate, stepwise approach to clinical development.

The Clinical Pipeline

The key to ORIC's pipeline philosophy is contained within the name itself — Overcoming Resistance in Cancer. The pipeline is not simply a series of individual assets, but rather a coordinated strategy which targets mechanisms through which cancerous tumors resist available treatments. The two leading programs in ORIC's pipeline — Rinzimetostat (ORIC-944) and Enozertinib (ORIC-114) — are each small molecule therapies designed to improve existing standards of care for patients with prostate cancer and non-small cell lung cancer respectively. Each therapy was designed to address pharmacologic shortcomings associated with current therapies such as poor bioavailability, inability to penetrate the CNS, and/or off target toxicity.

Rinzimetostat targets the Polycomb Repressive Complex 2 (PRC2), a major epigenetic regulatory mechanism involved in the lineage plasticity of prostate cancer cells. As tumors develop resistance to ARPIs, they often lose dependence on the androgen receptor pathway; in this state, the PRC2 is thought to be significantly involved. Therefore, inhibition of the PRC2, which rinzimetostat is designed to do, should restore sensitivity of the tumor to hormonal therapy.

Enozertinib targets the Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor. Specifically, it is designed to inhibit Exon 20 insertions and P-loop and alpha C-helix compressing mutations. A key feature of Enozertinib is that it has been engineered to have high potency against these difficult to target mutations while having strict selectivity to protect the wild-type EGFR from which many of the toxicities arise in other drugs and also to be able to penetrate the blood-brain barrier to provide treatment for brain metastases — a common sanctuary site for non-small cell lung cancer.

Rinzimetostat Opportunity

The recent rinzimetostat clinical update provided essential information about the drug’s progression. ORIC has transitioned past simply increasing the dose of the drug and selected provisional recommended Phase 2 dosing levels for use in combination with darolutamide and apalutamide.

When looking at the Phase 1b trial data in comparison to historical controls, the data indicates that for men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer who have previously failed an ARPI (androgen-receptor pathway inhibitor), the rinzimetostat combination treatments resulted in a PSA50 response rate of 55%. More compelling than the PSA50 response rate was the depth of these responses. Twenty percent of the men treated with the rinzimetostat combination had a PSA90 response. Deep biochemical responses in relapsed or refractory prostate cancer often correlate with survival advantages.

The best news came from the circulating tumor DNA analysis. ORIC announced that 59% of the men in the Phase 1b trial had a ctDNA clearance. As oncologists and regulators view ctDNA clearance as a leading indicator of potential long-term clinical benefit, this level of clearance is better than what has been observed historically in similar mCRPC patient populations in prior trials utilizing standard-of-care therapies (e.g., taxane chemotherapy or alternative ARPI switches).

The favorable safety profile demonstrated in the clinical update is also important. Since rinzimetostat will be used in combination with other medications that each have their own side effect profiles, and since many prostate cancer patients are older and may have additional medical conditions, the tolerability of rinzimetostat is a critical commercial element. The fact that rinzimetostat can be administered chronically at multiple dose levels without experiencing significant grade 3-4 side effects suggests that it could become a backbone therapy in the treatment paradigm for mCRPC. The first registrational Phase 3 study is scheduled to begin globally in the first half of 2026.

Enozertinib And The CNS Advantage

Based on the results of the Phase 1b trial for Enozertinib, 100% of patients treated in the trial achieved an intracranial objective response based on measurable CNS disease. These include patients with both active untreated brain metastasis. As stated earlier, this is an impressive statistic. With many of the other treatments available for patients with EGFR Exon 20 insertion mutations having limited ability to penetrate the blood-brain barrier, these treatments leave the brain as a sanctuary site where the disease can continue to grow and kill the patient, even though the systemic disease may be under control. Therefore, by demonstrating complete intracranial responses, Enozertinib addresses the most glaring limitation of the currently approved Exon 20 inhibitors.

Notably, the systemic efficacy of Enozertinib also appears to be highly competitive; a 67% objective response rate has been observed in the preliminary first-line Exon 20 cohort. The objective response rates reported for the competitive therapies have historically ranged from approximately 40% to 50% in this challenging mutation subtype. It should be noted that the company has optimized the dosing of Enozertinib, and selected 80 mg once daily as the Phase 3 dose. Although the 120 mg dose appeared to provide similar efficacy, the 120 mg dose required frequent dose reductions. However, the 80 mg dose provided similar efficacy as the 120 mg dose, but did so in a manner that resulted in improved tolerability, thereby reducing the risk associated with the expected future registrational trials with respect to safety.

The partnership between ORIC and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) to study the combination of Enozertinib and amivantamab represents a third-party validation of Enozertinib's potential. I believe their decision to supply their own antibody for use in combination with Enozertinib indicates that they do not see Enozertinib as simply a competitive product, but rather as a complementary agent to their own antibody that provides CNS exposure that amivantamab does not.

Counterarguments And Risks

I would say that the main risk to consider is the sample size. The samples (cohorts) creating this enthusiasm were generated by Phase 1b clinical trials. Specifically, the cohort used for the safety/efficacy analysis for rinzimetostat was comprised of n = 20 patients; whereas, the cohort used for the first line enozertinib analysis included n = 33 patients. Even though the results appear promising, smaller patient populations may create artificially high response rates, which can later drop back down as the number of patients increases in larger Phase 3 studies. There is no assurance that an overall response rate of 67% in lung cancer or a 55% proportion of patients who achieve a ≥ 50% decline in prostate specific antigen in prostate cancer will remain constant when increased to hundreds of patients across multiple locations globally.

In addition to the potential issues with sample size, the competitive environment in both indications is quite aggressive. Physicians treating patients with prostate cancer currently have many options available, including radioligand therapy and other novel androgen receptor degrader products. Therefore, not only does rinzimetostat need to demonstrate it is safe and effective, but it needs to be shown to be safer and/or more effective than other products in order to capture market share. Although enozertinib appears to be more favorable than competing products in terms of CNS penetration, there are several large pharmaceutical companies actively working on next-generation tyrosine kinase inhibitor products for the treatment of EGFR exon 20 mutations. Thus, if one of these competitors reaches the market before ORIC, the company's commercial opportunity could be significantly reduced.

Addressable Market And Competitive Landscape

ORIC’s assets represent a very large total addressable market. For example, the mCRPC population post-abiraterone or enzalutamide represents a multi-billion dollar market opportunity in prostate cancer. ORIC has estimated the U.S. addressable market for rinzimetostat at greater than $7 billion, focused exclusively on the previously treated population. However, there is significant potential for growth by transitioning to earlier lines of therapy for mCSPC; the volume of patients in this category is manyfold larger than in the previously treated space. As the mechanism of action of PRC2 inhibition theoretically prevents the lineage plasticity that is the basis for drug-resistant cells, rinzimetostat may one day be able to serve as a maintenance therapy in earlier stage disease.

For lung cancer, while the EGFR Exon 20 and PACC mutation markets are smaller than the classical EGFR mutation market, they offer high-value opportunities. ORIC estimates a potential annual U.S. commercial opportunity for enozertinib of approximately $3.0 to $3.5 billion. There are few treatment options available today for patients who harbor these mutations, with chemotherapy or first generation agents offering little in terms of efficacy, and/or poor tolerability. The PACC mutation market in particular is an emerging market with no approved targeted therapies for this segment. This represents an amazing opportunity for enozertinib, should ORIC obtain the first approval for this indication.

Future Outlook And Conclusion

From what I see, ORIC has removed many of the technical and financial obstacles that often hinder the progress of biotechnology companies at the Phase 2/Phase 3 stage. By identifying and targeting the biological root causes of drug failure (i.e., CNS sanctuary sites in lung cancer and lineage plasticity in prostate cancer), ORIC is addressing the primary reasons why existing drugs fail. With a sufficient amount of time and funding remaining to complete the Phase 3 programs, and assuming investors can tolerate the risks associated with those studies, I believe the current risk/reward ratio appears attractive, and I will be watching the trial outcomes closely.

data.title: Stratasys: Mass Adoption Concerns Remain
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to take a look at Stratasys Ltd.’s (SSYS) performance for the three quarters of 2025 and give some comments on whether I think there is a possibility that the company could be added to my portfolio. So far, profitability eludes the company, but given its massive cash pile, it has ample time to adjust its cost strategy and hope that the 3D printing process will see mass adoption in the next few years.

Performance

The company’s sales have been on a downtrend throughout the last couple of years. It has been steadily declining around 2% every quarter, which isn’t ideal, but not the end of the world either. The company guided for FY25 to be in the range of $550-$560m, which means it’ll be down 3%-5% compared to FY24, so there is no turnaround in sight just yet. We’ll have to wait for the management’s commentary when the company hosts the Q4 conference call in March.

During the same period, we can see that the company’s margin profile across the board has somewhat improved from FY24; however, the company is still rather deep in the red, and profitability doesn’t seem to be in sight. At least not for 2026. We can also see that gross margins began to trend down throughout 2025 due to the macroeconomic uncertainty and the impact of tariffs. In the most recent quarter, the company’s gross margins eroded by almost 400bps in GAAP terms, and 430bps in non-GAAP terms. GAAP operating loss narrowed slightly to $22.7m from $25.5m, and non-GAAP operating income eked out $100k in earnings compared to a $100k loss last year. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $5m, and non-GAAP net income was also positive at $1.5m or 2 cents per share. On a TTM basis, adjusted EBITDA margin remains negative, which means net income is also still negative.

So, the company is still struggling to become profitable, but it has a lot of time to get everything in order going forward, given its rather strong financial position. As of the latest quarter, SSYS had around $71.5m in cash and equivalents, as well as $183.5m in ST investments, giving over $254m in liquidity available. This cash pile goes well with not having any long-term debt, so the company can focus on improving the product, hone in on its cost structure a bit more, and invest in the future growth overall, without worrying about any sort of debt covenants it needs to adhere to.

Additionally, the company is operating cash flow positive as of 9 months ended September, so it has essentially no cash burn, further proving it has ample time to eventually get to profitability, and on its own terms.

Overall, the company is heading in the right direction, it’s just taking a while to turn a profit, which makes me a bit worried given the macroeconomic outlook right now and the overall product adoptability.

3D printing is surging in popularity

You don’t hear much about 3D printing recently. The fad faded a few years ago, and now the segment is becoming a lot more serious when it comes to manufacturing viability. It seems that 3D printed materials have such a wide variety of applications that it just makes sense for many companies to try to adopt such techniques into their manufacturing process. However, the issue remains with these companies to properly market their product and show that it is indeed a much better process than what is currently available to the end-customers.

The 3D printing market is projected to grow from $28.4B in 2026 to $59.24B by 2032, giving us a very respectable 12.93% CAGR in 6 years. This is really good; however, it seems that SSYS may have a tough time capturing a decent share of that growth. At least that is what I see right now. I am looking forward to hearing what the management has to say regarding the outlook for the next couple of quarters or for all of 2026, and whether we will see a decent rebound in top-line growth.

The pivot to manufacturing is going to help it expand its margins considerably if successful, since prototyping was more of a quick, low-volume 3D print for design validation and testing early in product development. Now, with such a pivot to manufacturing, the company will be able to focus on key verticals like Aerospace, automotive, and dental, which are key growth segments for 3D printing.

So, I expect to see a decent rejuvenation in the company’s operations. Sales should begin to grow, maybe even at low double-digits and decent margin expansion that can push it into a profitable territory sometime in 2027. I don’t expect much to change in 2026, but I wouldn’t mind being proven wrong here if the company can turn a GAAP profit by the end of 2026.

Viability concerns remain

As I mentioned earlier, it is taking a long time for manufacturing companies to open to the possibility that the way they are producing their product could be produced more efficiently via 3D printing. Many manufacturing industries are deeply ingrained in their current process, which has a proven track record and scalability, so it is going to be hard to tell them that there may be a better way of doing this. This will require a complete rebuild of their manufacturing processes, and that is one of the reasons companies may be ignoring it. The capex to change the whole process is probably too much to even tackle.

In industries like aerospace and automotive, there are a lot more regulatory approvals necessary, and certification is vital to prove that 3D printed parts are not only as good as the current way the product is made, but also better and stronger/safer.

However, some parts of these critical industries, like aerospace, automotive, and dentistry, are increasingly taking 3D printing more seriously for applications that do not necessarily require a lot of approvals from regulators and are used for non-critical purposes. For example, interiors and cabin panels, ducting, brackets, and clamps in aerospace. Tooling, fixtures, and spares for automotive, dentures, surgical guides, and anatomic models for dentistry. So, if the company can gain momentum in these non-critical applications, I could see it performing well.

Investor Takeaway

The mass adoptability of 3D printing parts remains an issue in my view. I would like to see how this story develops over the next few quarters and whether the projections, which are quite optimistic in my view, will indeed come true, and whether SSYS can capture a decent chunk of that growth going forward.

For now, I don’t see myself adding SSYS to my portfolio, as the prospects seem to be a little thin regarding 2026.

data.title: Weekly Treasury Simulation, January 16, 2026: Most Likely Range For 3-Month T-Bill Rate 10 Years Forward Unchanged At 1% To 2%
data.description:
As explained in Prof. Robert Jarrow’s book cited below, forward rates contain a risk premium above and beyond the market’s expectations for the 3-month forward rate. We document the size of that risk premium in this graph, which shows the zero-coupon yield curve implied by current Treasury prices compared with the annualized compounded yield on 3-month Treasury bills that market participants would expect based on the daily movement of government bond yields in 14 countries since 1962. The risk premium, the reward for a long-term investment, is large and widens over most of the full 30-year maturity range. The graph also shows a decline in expected yields at a steady pace for the full 30 years. We explain the details below.

For more on this topic, see the analysis of government bond yields in 14 countries through November 30, 2025, given in the appendix.

Inverted Yields, Negative Rates, and U.S. Treasury Probabilities 10 Years Forward

The negative 2-year/10-year Treasury spread ended on August 26, 2024, after 537 trading days. The spread is currently at positive 0.65%. The table below shows that the August 26, 2024, streak of inverted yield curves is the longest in the U.S. Treasury market since the 2-year Treasury yield was first reported on June 1, 1976. The second longest streak is 423 trading days, starting on August 18, 1978.

In this week’s forecast, the focus is on three elements of interest rate behavior: the future probability of the recession-predicting inverted yield curve, the probability of negative rates, and the probability distribution of U.S. Treasury yields over the next decade.

We start from the closing U.S. Treasury yield curve published daily by the U.S. Department of the Treasury. Using a maximum smoothness forward rate approach, Friday’s implied forward rate curve shows the shortest maturity 1-month forward rates at 3.75%, versus 3.70% last week. There is a decline followed by a gradual rise until rates peak at 5.97%, unchanged from the previous week. Forward rates stabilize at 4.56% at the 30-year horizon, also unchanged from last week.

Using the methodology outlined in the appendix, we simulate 50,000 future paths for the U.S. Treasury yield curve out to thirty years. The next three sections summarize our conclusions from that simulation.

Inverted Treasury Yields: Positive Spread Now, 24.7% Probability by March 30, 2040

Many economists have concluded that a downward-sloping U.S. Treasury yield curve is an important indicator of future recessions. A recent example is this paper by Alex Domash and Lawrence H. Summers. We measure the probability that the 10-year par coupon Treasury yield is lower than the 2-year par coupon Treasury for every scenario in each of the first 40 quarterly periods in the simulation [1]. The next graph shows that the probability of an inverted yield now peaks at 24.7%, unchanged from one week before, in the 91-day period ending March 30, 2040.

Negative Treasury Bill Yields: 11.3% Probability by December 20, 2047

The next graph describes the probability of negative 3-month Treasury bill rates for all but the first 3 months of the next three decades. The probability of negative rates starts near zero and then peaks at 11.3% in the 91-day period ending December 20, 2047.

Calculating the Default Risk from Interest Rate Maturity Mismatches

In light of the interest-rate-risk-driven failure of Silicon Valley Bank on March 10, 2023, we have added a table that applies equally well to banks, institutional investors, and individual investor mismatches from buying long-term Treasury bonds with borrowed short-term funds. We assume that the sole asset is a 10-year Treasury bond purchased at time zero at a par value of $100. We analyze default risk for four different initial market value of equity to market value of asset ratios: 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%. For the banking example, we assume that the only class of liabilities is deposits that can be withdrawn at par at any time. In the institutional and retail investor case, we assume that the liability is essentially a borrowing on margin/repurchase agreement with the possibility of margin calls. For all investors, the amount of liabilities (95, 90, 85, or 80) represents a “strike price” on a put option held by the liability holders. Failure occurs via a margin call, bank run, or regulatory takeover (in the banking case) when the value of assets falls below the value of liabilities.

The graph below shows the cumulative 10-year probabilities of failure for each of the 4 possible capital ratios when the asset’s maturity is 10 years. For the 5 percent case, that default probability is 49.18%, compared to 49.31% from last week.

This default probability analysis is updated weekly based on the U.S. Treasury yield simulation described in the next section. The calculation process is the same for any portfolio of assets with credit risk included.

U.S. Treasury Probabilities 10 Years Forward

In this section, the focus turns to the decade ahead. This week’s simulation shows that the most likely range for the 3-month U.S. Treasury bill yield in ten years is from 1% to 2%, unchanged from last week. There is a 22.26% probability that the 3-month yield falls in this range, compared to 22.22% one week before. For the 10-year Treasury yield, the most likely range is from 2% to 3%, unchanged from last week. The probability of being in this range is 21.18%, compared to 21.13% one week prior.

In a recent post on SeekingAlpha, we pointed out that a forecast of “heads” or “tails” in a coin flip leaves out critical information. What a sophisticated bettor needs to know is that, on average for a fair coin, the probability of heads is 50%. A forecast that the next coin flip will be “heads” is literally worth nothing to investors because the outcome is purely random.

The same is true for interest rates.

In this section we present the detailed probability distribution for both the 3-month Treasury bill rate and the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield 10 years forward using semi-annual time steps [2]. We present the probability of where rates will be at each time step in one percent “rate buckets.” The forecast for 3-month Treasury yields is shown in this graph:

3-Month U.S. Treasury Yield Data Are Attached.

SAS3monthUST20260116.xlsx

The probability that the 3-month Treasury bill yield will be between 1% and 2% in 2 years is shown in column 4: 25.14%. The probability that the 3-month Treasury bill yield will be negative (as it has been often in Europe and Japan) in 2 years is 1.64% plus 0.05% plus 0.00% = 1.69% (difference due to rounding). Cells shaded in blue represent positive probabilities of occurring, but the probability has been rounded to the nearest 0.01%. The shading scheme is defined as follows:

  • Dark blue: the probability is greater than 0% but less than 1%
  • Light blue: the probability is greater than or equal to 1% and less than 5%
  • Light yellow: the probability is greater than or equal to 5% and 10%
  • Medium yellow: the probability is greater than or equal to 10% and less than 20%
  • Orange: the probability is greater than or equal to 20% and less than 25%
  • Red: the probability is greater than 25%

The chart below shows the same probabilities for the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield derived as part of the same simulation.

10-Year US Treasury Yield Data Are Attached.

SAS10yearUST20260116.xlsx

Modeling Correlated Foreign Exchange Rates and Government Yields

The SAS HJM++© methodology is based on five decades of research by Prof. Robert Jarrow. Using the no-arbitrage conditions of Heath, Jarrow, and Morton [1992] and foreign exchange rate no-arbitrage conditions from Amin and Jarrow [1992], foreign exchange rates and government yields in major markets are simulated jointly. The simulation is based on daily movements in government yields and foreign exchange rates since January 2, 1962. The resulting distribution of the yen-U.S. dollar exchange rate one year forward is shown in this graph:

Similarly, the forward-looking correlation between 10-year zero-coupon Bund and U.S. Treasury yields is shown below:

For more information on HJM++ scenario generation and no-arbitrage Monte Carlo simulation, please contact the author.

Appendix: Treasury Simulation Methodology

The Treasury yield probabilities are derived using the same methodology that SAS Institute Inc. recommends to its KRIS® and SAS ALM/Kamakura Risk Manager® clients. A moderately technical explanation is given later in the appendix, but we summarize it in plain English first.

Step 1: We take the closing U.S. Treasury yield curve as our starting point.

Step 2: We use the number of points on the yield curve that best explains historical yield curve shifts. Using daily government bond yield data from 14 countries from 1962 through November 30, 2025, we conclude that 12 “factors” drive almost all movements of government bond yields. The countries on which the analysis is based are Australia, Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, New Zealand. Russia, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Thailand, the United Kingdom, and the United States of America. No data from Russia is included after January 2022. The graph below shows which points on the zero-coupon yield curve are incorporated as risk factors and the order in which they are added to the model:

Step 3: We measure the volatility of changes in those factors and how volatility has changed over the same period.

Step 4: Using those measured volatilities, we generate 50,000 random shocks at each time step and derive the resulting yield curve.

Step 5: We “validate” the model to make sure that the simulation EXACTLY prices the starting Treasury curve and that it fits history as well as possible. The methodology for doing this is described below.

Step 6: We take all 50,000 simulated yield curves and calculate the probabilities that yields fall in each of the 1% “buckets” displayed in the graph.

Do Treasury Yields Accurately Reflect Expected Future Inflation?

We showed in a recent post on Seeking Alpha that, on average, investors have almost always done better by buying long-term bonds than by rolling over short-term Treasury bills. That means that market participants have generally (but not always) been accurate in forecasting future inflation and adding a risk premium to that forecast.

The distribution above helps investors estimate the probability of success from going long.

Finally, as mentioned weekly in the Corporate Bond Investor Friday overview, the future expenses (both the amount and the timing) that all investors are trying to cover with their investments are an important part of investment strategy. The author follows his own advice: cover the short-term cash needs first and then step out to cover more distant cash needs as savings and investment returns accumulate.

Technical Details

Daily government bond yields from the 14 countries listed above form the base historical data for fitting the number of yield curve factors and their volatility. The U.S. historical data is provided by the U.S. Department of the Treasury. The use of the international bond data increases the number of observations to more than 109,000 and provides a more complete range of experience with both high rates and negative rates than a U.S. data set alone provides.

The modeling process was published in a very important paper by David Heath, Robert Jarrow, and Andrew Morton in 1992:

For technically inclined readers, we recommend Prof. Jarrow’s book Modeling Fixed Income Securities and Interest Rate Options for those who want to know exactly how the “HJM” model construction works.

The number of factors, 12 for the 14-country model, has been stable since June 30, 2017.

Footnotes:

[1] After the first 20 years in the simulation, the 10-year Treasury cannot be derived from the initial 30 years of Treasury yields.

[2] The full simulation uses 91-day time steps for 30 years forward. This note summarizes just the first 10 years of the full simulation.

data.title: U.S.-Europe Trade War Over Greenland: What Investors Need To Know
data.description:
It seems as though there is never a dull moment in the markets with the current Administration.

The latest battle has been over the future of Greenland, which is an autonomous region of Denmark. President Trump fired a weekend broadside on Saturday, 17th January.

In a tweet on ‘Truth Social,’ Trump announced the imposition of a 10% tariff on European countries that are resisting his efforts to take over the sovereignty of Greenland. These include Germany, France, the United Kingdom, Denmark, Norway, Sweden, the Netherlands, and Finland.

True to the playbook, the initial 10% tariff is accompanied by a threat for an escalation to 25% if the matter is not resolved by June 1st.

Importantly, this is not an attack on the European Union. The tariffs are aimed specifically at the NATO members who have been most vocal in pointing out that their obligations under the NATO Treaty oblige them to act to protect the sovereignty and territory of a member state - in this case, Denmark.

This twist in the Greenland issue is becoming a threat not just to NATO, but to the EU. By targeting individual member states of the EU but not the EU itself, Trump has raised an issue that could be divisive in formulating a cohesive response.

The political considerations and ramifications of this development are huge, but as this is an investing site, I'll focus on the economic and market impacts.

European Response

Over the weekend, the early response from the leaders of the targeted countries has been indignant. The key European leaders have pushed back.

In a collective response, leaders of the countries targeted issued a joint communique.

This pledged full solidarity with Greenland and warned that Trumps action's threatened "undermine transatlantic relations and risk a dangerous downward spiral."

U.K. Prime Minister Starmer called the move "completely wrong". He had a call with President Trump scheduled for Sunday, 18th, to discuss.

At the EU level, ministers gathered for an emergency summit over the weekend, and are reported to have reached "broad agreement" over the response, with a follow-up meeting scheduled for Thursday, 22nd.

EU Tariff Package

The simplest measure, as reported by Reuters, is a EUR 93bn ($108bn) package of tariffs on US imports, which is agreed but on hold following the draft trade agreement with the US from last year.

These have been suspended for six months pending formalization of the trade agreement but will automatically commence on 6th February unless the EU takes action to cancel them.

The tariff package is aimed at a broad spectrum of US goods, including aircraft, cars, wine, and medical and electrical tools.

So this seems to be a 'no brainer' as there needs to be no further action from the EU for this retaliation to come into effect just five days after the new US tariff package.

The 'Bazooka'

A more complex topic will be the implementation of "The Bazooka" anti-coercion measures formally known as the ACI.

Described in detail here, these measures were adopted by the EU in 2023 but have never been used. This is a broad range of economic penalties to be used against countries deemed as coercing EU member states through economic means. According to Wikipedia:

Under the regulation, "economic coercion" refers to a situation in which a third country seeks to pressure the European Union or a member state into making a particular policy choice by applying, or threatening to apply, measures affecting trade or investment

So it seems an easy case to make that the latest US tariffs would qualify. The measures are very broad but include trade sanctions and financial controls, freezing intellectual property rights, limiting financial market access, plus measures against direct investment in the target nation.

Importantly, the ACI removes the need for a unanimous EU vote but instead can be enacted by a qualified majority of EU members.

Ironically, the ACI was designed with China as the aggressor in mind. Even as recently as 2023, the situation today could not be imagined.

The French leader Emmanuel Macron has already pushed for formal discussions on implementing the ACI.

The United Kingdom

Another big protagonist here is the UK. Since Brexit, of course, the UK has not been subject to EU trade actions, so the $108bn EU tariff package excludes the UK. The UK imported GBP 111bn ($150m) of goods from the US in the year to September 2025.

Prime Minister Keir Starmer held a press conference on 19th January, in which he reiterated the UK's support for Denmark as the sovereign of Greenland and NATO as the conduit for ensuring the security of Greenland.

Initial comments made by opposition leaders to the US tariff threats showed bipartisan support for a strong response. Conservative Party leader Kemi Badenoch said the tariffs were a "terrible idea". Starmer thanked her for her support during the press conference.

However, Starmer stressed that a trade war would be against the interests of the UK, and he hoped to avoid this.

So all in all, there seems to be broad EU alignment and the intention to respond with at least retaliatory tariffs, plus a ready-to-roll mechanism for broader measures. The UK seems to be treading a softer path.

The Treasury Market

Another potential market disruption is the large amount of US debt that is held by European countries. In total, according to Deutsche Bank, European nations own $8 trillion in US Treasuries.

Deutsche speculate that US treasury sales could be the largest ammunition with which to load the "Bazooka". This clearly represents a short-term threat to US Treasury prices, domestic interest rates, and the US economy.

In the longer term, regardless of the outcome of the coming months, it is likely that deploying this policy tool will have a negative long-term impact on European holding of US treasuries.

European fiscal policy embeds holding US treasuries as a key part of the deal struck in the post war era, which involves the USD as the global reserve currency and mutual security through NATO.

With key components of this 'deal' starting to unravel, the necessity of holding US treasuries is reduced. An obvious longer-term impact is that US treasury reserves are likely to be sold down.

According to Economic Insider, as of November 2025, the picture was as shown below.

The UK, France, and Norway are all major holders. Germany is not, but still holds $100bn.

Note also the large holding from Canada. Mark Carney has been vocal in his support of Denmark, and Canada has been working hard not just to strengthen ties with the EU, but build new ones with previous foes, such as China. An asset shift away from US treasuries seems likely.

Sectors To Watch

Treasuries

So the number on asset at risk seems to be US treasuries, particularly long bonds (TLT). Prices had been trying to make a recovery in the back half of 2025, but this stalled in November.

Year to date, there has been another attempt to rally. I expect downward pressure to continue.

The Dollar (DXY)

The dollar follows a similar course, showing strength from October but losing gains into the year end. Despite a recovery year to date, DXY is close to the 52 week low. I expect to see DXY weakness resume.

Broad Equities (SPY)

SPY has gained 1.4% year to date, and is at the top of the 52 week trading range. Concerns around valuations and the Federal Reserve independence have been in play in the last week.

As of the time of writing S&P futures were down by 1%. I expect to see continued volatility. The market seems to be looking for an excuse to correct. This could be the trigger, but I for one doubt that unless there is a major escalation from the EU.

Gold

Gold (GLD) I expect to benefit. Firstly, as a safe haven asset, gold generally catches a bid when geopolitical tensions rise. We are clearly seeing that here. Secondly, from a broader fiscal sense. The potential inflationary effects of renewed, and possibly expanded tariffs, and dollar weakness should support gold prices. Thirdly, if, as discussed above, there is a US treasury sell down from Europe, Gold is likely to increase in demand.

GLD has enjoyed close to 70% gains in the last year. As discussed here, this can be expected to continue in 2026.

As at the time of writing, GLD futures are up 1.5%.

Conclusion

The tariff threat is a characteristically aggressive move from President Trump. It represents an escalation of a foreign policy that has been undermining relations with previously close allies, near and far.

Early indications are that Europe is resolved to push back if necessary, however some, like Starmer, are clearly nervous about a full blown trade war. Others, such as Macron seem to be willing to escalate and deploy powerful EU counter-measures.

World and business leaders are gathered in Davos, Switzerland this week, and there is likely to be intense dialogue.

The short-term risk is of an escalating trade war, and the inevitable economic and market damage of that.

The long-term risk is continued pressure on US debt, the dollar, inflation and interest rates.

The key investment theme from the current situation is short DXY & TLT - long GLD. Equities remain on watch, with another reason to worry added to the list.

data.title: Micron: Peak Cycle Margins And Unprecedented Capex Risk
data.description:
Introduction

Micron Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ: MU) has probably been one of the best performers on the market since I last gave it a Hold rating back in September. The stock has appreciated by over 131% based on various key decisions the company has made and the relentless rise in prices. With that in mind, I want to make the case for why I believe the valuation might have gone too far.

Right now, the company designs, manufactures, and sells memory and storage products worldwide and has been surfing the boom in demand for memories and HBMs based on the rise of AI.

Current Dynamics

The first recent major piece of news that we can substantially decipher would be the winding down of its consumer-centered SSD and RAM business, as to be able to shift production capacity toward high-margin AI data center products. The Crucial brand has been known as the gold standard for reliability, and the move directly tells us that Micron wants to instantly capitalize on the AI gold rush instead of its legacy business. Economically speaking, the move is understandable as HBM currently sells for a massive premium compared to a regular $50 stick of desktop RAM. Right now, Silicon wafer production is a zero-sum game as the insatiable demand from data centers forces producers to choose between production capacities. Micron won’t technically fully exit consumer RAM as it will still provide brands like Corsair and Kingston, but only after their high-priority AI and enterprise customers are satisfied. I believe that the move is inherently positive based on the current dynamics of the market, as Micron will focus on profitability over volume, pushing gross margins to nearly 60% in a commodity style of product segment.

I won’t do a full dive into the company’s last earnings as it is already old news, but it is important to highlight the fundamental shift in the company’s financial DNA. As it is prioritizing high-value AI components, the company has been able to achieve hyper-growth levels of profitability. The company has stated that its entire HBM supply for 2026 is already sold out, giving the company incredible pricing power, as highlighted by the incredible rise in prices. See production of HBM takes up around three times the wafer capacity of standard DDR5 RAM, which creates a voluntary shortage in the PC and smartphone markets, allowing prices to rise for consumer chips. This has pushed the company to make a shocking guidance with expected revenue set to reach $18.7B and gross margin to reach 68%, the highest level seen for the company and probably the industry.

And now the most recent rally could be related to the $100B megafab located in Clay, breaking ground that will be the company’s crown jewel of its domestic strategy, now being the largest private construction project in New York state history. The project will eventually house 4 fabs and will focus its production on leading-edge DRAM, the primary source of HBM that powers AI servers and next-generation cloud infrastructure. The project will be highly subsidized by the CHIPS and Science Act in a bid to push the country’s DRAM production to 40% of the global production within the next decade, taming away a crucial risk in the current AI supply chain.

All of these are positives in a way, but I believe that there is a potential substantial risk in betting the whole house on HBM for AI. First, if the industry cools down, then Micron won't easily flip a switch and revert to making consumer RAM. If, in the end, prices drop for Micron, then they will be left with a massive facility that produces 66% less stuff than it used to (hence the 3x wafer capacity required). More so, the company is spending $100B over 20 years, while semiconductor cycles been notoriously boom and bust. If the next phase of AI, deemed Agentic AI, does not produce immediate profits for companies like Microsoft and Google, the demand for $10,000 HBM modules will slow down. And then the company will be stuck with a half-finished 20-year project. Now, MU has shut down its shock absorber in Crucial, meaning that it has nowhere to dump extra inventory if its big enterprise partners suddenly cut orders. It has essentially cut their diverse customer base for a handful of hyperscalers, implying a huge concentration risk. The megafab is also making Micron now politically tied to the US government trade wars. If they are banned from selling certain tech to specific global markets, they won’t easily change their production elsewhere.

Valuation

In a world of expensive AI-related tech companies, I’ll say that Micron has a slightly too expensive valuation based on its P/E GAAP of 34.42, which is highly above the company’s historical average as the company pivoted towards AI. But as highlighted by the crash of 2023 that wiped out years of gains, there is the possibility of huge busts on the horizon for Micron, especially as its stock price becomes that much heightened. Just like in 2021, I believe that we’re in a panic-buying phase where companies like Nvidia and Amazon are terrified of running out of memory, but if they realize that they overbuilt their AI data center, then they will suddenly cancel orders where Micron would be left with billions in unsold, highly specialized HBM4 memory. Furthermore, Micron wants to spend $20B a year in CapEx, mainly for fabs. But if there were to be an AI winter, Micron would be stuck with $100B half-finished factories and no way to pay debt, while it is currently eating into its cash position.

In the near term, I expect growth to be tremendous for the company, as it has officially sold out its entire HBM capacity through the end of 2026, with customers like Nvidia and AMD only getting 50% to 66% of the memory they actually want from Micron. Moreover, Micron currently has HBM4 dominance, as they were the first to move the next-gen memory into mass production, capturing the highest-margin slice of the AI market before Samsung or SK Hynix can fully catch up. The Megafab that is poised to alleviate the manufacturing issues is a 20-year build-out that is already seeing signs of real-world friction with delays in production years and labor shortage, as it requires a large number of specialized workers. That’s why Micron fast-tracked its Boise, Idaho fab, which is expected to come online much sooner but at a greater cost at a time of already high CapEx.

Micron’s profitability right now is off the roof, to be fair, as the company is benefitting from extremely high-demand and limited supply capacity, but again, I do not expect these dynamics to continue for decades, while the company is planning it to. I expect rivals such as Samsung to ramp up their own AI memory production, hitting Micron’s current dominant position. The company is expecting to hit full volume in late 2026, while they will likely start a price war to gain market share, in my opinion, forcing Micron's margins back down, and I do not believe that the market has been pricing this for the company. That’s why I do not believe that MU has a real Moat protecting its margins, as it is only protected by a supply shortage and not better technology that will be essentially caught up by competitors.

Conclusion

Micron is a great company, yes, and in a sense, the valuation metrics right now could make the case for an investment. But diving into the details and the fundamentals, I do not see how investors should invest in the company at over $360 a share. Competition is prone to catch up at a time when it is spending left and right to capture the most of a transitory period. In that sense, I would recommend shareholders to sell their shares right now, hence I give a Sell rating for Micron Technology.

data.title: Alger Capital Appreciation Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Update

The following segment was excerpted from the Alger Capital Appreciation Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Portfolio Update

Class A shares of the Alger Capital Appreciation Fund underperformed the Russell 1000 Growth Index during the fourth quarter of 2025. Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), Western Digital Corporation (WDC), and Natera, Inc. (NTRA) were among the top contributors to performance.

Alphabet is the parent of Google and a global leader in digital advertising, cloud computing, and AI-driven consumer services. Shares contributed positively during the quarter as investors increasingly recognized improving execution across the company’s AI strategy, supported by strong engagement trends and rising AI-related workloads. Google Cloud was a key driver, with management highlighting accelerating usage, including increased AI token processing and continued momentum in the Gemini ecosystem. Sentiment was further supported by strengthening cloud demand and new customer wins, as well as reports that Google may broaden access to its TPU capabilities, including potential deployments beyond Google Cloud.

Western Digital is a hard disk drive (HDD) storage company benefiting from rising hyperscaler data-center investment and the continued proliferation of data. The HDD industry is highly consolidated, with only two scaled manufacturers, and Western Digital holds a leading market position. The business has structurally shifted toward cloud customers as consumer exposure has declined, with cloud representing the majority of Western Digital’s revenue. Importantly, industry participants have emphasized capital discipline—prioritizing higher areal density (i.e., more terabytes per drive) rather than adding significant unit capacity—which supports a healthier supply/demand balance and improved profitability. During the quarter, shares contributed positively as the company reported operating results that validated improving fundamentals, including strong revenue and earnings growth driven by higher unit volumes, better-than-guided drive density, favorable pricing, and lower cost per terabyte that helped expand margins.

Natera is a specialty diagnostics laboratory that provides high-value genetic testing across three core franchises: reproductive health (including non-invasive prenatal testing for chromosomal conditions such as trisomy 13, 18, and 21), oncology (led by Signatera, which measures circulating tumor DNA to assess treatment response and detect molecular residual disease and cancer recurrence), and transplant (tests used to monitor organ rejection). These tests are built on the company’s proprietary cell-free DNA liquid biopsy platform, enabling highly sensitive detection from a blood sample. Shares contributed positively after the company reported strong fiscal third-quarter earnings, with management commentary pointing to revenue and margin performance that exceeded expectations on the back of higher test volumes and favorable reimbursement dynamics— particularly within the Signatera franchise—alongside another increase to full-year 2025 revenue and margin guidance.

Nebius Group (NBIS), Meta Platforms Inc (META), and Sea Limited (SE) were among the top detractors from performance.

Nebius is a provider of AI-focused cloud infrastructure, operating GPU compute capacity across Europe and expanding in the U.S. We view the company as a differentiated “neocloud” beneficiary of accelerating AI adoption, as demand for high-quality GPU compute continues to outstrip available supply of chips, power, and data center capacity. Nebius already operates a first-party data center in Finland and has deployed GPU capacity in Paris, while adding U.S. capacity through a Kansas City, Missouri deployment and a new data center build-out in New Jersey. During the quarter, shares detracted amid a broader selloff in AI infrastructure-related equities as investors grew more concerned about the “circularity” of AI ecosystem financing and the durability of AI infrastructure spending. Despite near-term volatility, Nebius continues to sign large, long-term agreements with tier-one customers, including multi-year AI infrastructure contracts with major cloud service providers, improving revenue visibility as new capacity comes online.

Meta is the world’s largest social-media company, operating platforms that include Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger. Shares detracted during the quarter as investors focused on management’s guidance for materially higher operating expenses and capital expenditures (capex) tied to AI infrastructure investments. Management noted that 2026 CapEx is expected to be “notably” higher than 2025, which pressured forward earnings and free-cash-flow expectations despite strong underlying results. While the company highlighted continued advertising momentum and indicated that revenue growth could remain robust into year-end, investors remained concerned about the magnitude and timing of the AI-related CapEx.

Sea Limited is a global consumer internet company operating three core businesses: Garena, a digital entertainment platform known for developing and publishing online games; Shopee, a leading e-commerce platform in Southeast Asia and Taiwan; and SeaMoney, which offers digital financial services including mobile wallets and payment processing. The company has benefited from strong structural growth in its e-commerce and payments business, along with its gaming business returning to stabilization after years of decline. Shares detracted from performance as investors questioned the pace and durability of Sea’s profitability improvement. Specifically, its largest e-commerce competitor in Latin America lowered free-shipping thresholds, which may increase competitive intensity and pressure margins in the near- term.
